seo.title: Ayutthaya Travel Guide: Everything You Need to Know (2026)
seo.metaDescription: The complete Ayutthaya travel guide for 2026. 2-day itinerary, transport from Bangkok, best temples, roti sai mai, budget breakdown and everything you need to plan your visit to Thailand's ancient capital.

intro: Ayutthaya was the capital of Siam for 417 years (1350–1767) and at its peak was one of the largest cities in the world, with over a million inhabitants trading with China, Japan, India, Persia, Portugal, France and the Netherlands. The Burmese army burned it to the ground in 1767, and the ruins that remain — now a UNESCO World Heritage Site — are among the most evocative in Southeast Asia. Located just 80 km north of Bangkok, Ayutthaya can be done as a day trip but is far better experienced over two days. This guide covers everything you need to plan a visit.

itinerary.title: 2 Days in Ayutthaya: The Complete Itinerary

days.title: The UNESCO Temple Circuit
activities: 07:00 — Catch the early train from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong station (3rd class 15–20 THB). Buy ticket at the counter — no advance booking needed.
09:00 — Arrive Ayutthaya station. Take the ferry (5 THB) across the river to the island. Rent a bicycle (50–100 THB/day) from shops near the ferry landing on Naresuan Road.
09:30 — Wat Mahathat: Start at the iconic Buddha head in tree roots before the day-trip crowds arrive from Bangkok (50 THB or 220 THB combo ticket for six temples).
10:30 — Wat Ratchaburana: Cross the road and descend into the crypt to see 15th-century frescoes (50 THB or combo ticket).
11:30 — Wat Phra Si Sanphet & Viharn Phra Mongkhon Bophit: Explore the three royal chedis and the massive bronze Buddha (50 THB + free).
12:30 — Lunch: Cycle to the Chao Phrom Market area for boat noodles (10–15 THB per bowl, order 3–5) or rice dishes (40–60 THB).
14:00 — Chao Sam Phraya National Museum: Air-conditioned respite from the midday heat; see the gold treasures from Wat Ratchaburana (150 THB).
15:30 — Wat Phra Ram: Peaceful ruins reflected in a lotus pond, usually uncrowded (50 THB or combo ticket).
17:00 — Cycle to Wat Chaiwatthanaram for sunset — Ayutthaya's most photogenic moment (50 THB or combo ticket).
18:30 — Return to the island for dinner at the Night Market near Chao Phrom — grilled meats, som tum and fresh fruit shakes (40–100 THB per dish).

days.title: Beyond the Main Circuit
activities: 07:30 — Early morning at Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon: Rows of saffron-draped Buddhas in golden light, climb the great chedi for panoramic views (20 THB).
09:00 — Wat Panang Choeng: Active riverside temple with a colossal 19-metre gilded Buddha — a centre of Thai-Chinese worship (20 THB).
10:30 — Cycle north to Wat Phu Khao Thong (Golden Mount): White chedi rising from rice paddies with 360-degree views — almost no tourists (free).
12:00 — Lunch at a riverside restaurant on the east bank — order giant river prawns grilled over charcoal (200–500 THB) with som tum and sticky rice.
13:30 — Roti sai mai tasting on Bang Ian Road: Watch vendors hand-pull candy floss threads and wrap them in thin roti (10–30 THB per portion). Buy bags to take home.

14:30 — Option A: Take a minivan or taxi to Bang Pa-In Royal Palace (20 km south, 100 THB entrance, 1.5–2 hours). Option B: Explore the Ayutthaya Floating Market (200 THB entrance) and take a boat ride.
17:00 — Return to the island. Pack up and catch the train back to Bangkok (last trains around 18:00–19:00, 1.5–2 hours).
Alternative: If staying a second night, take a sunset river cruise (200–500 THB) to see illuminated temples from the water.

transport.fromBangkok: Ayutthaya is 80 km north of Bangkok — one of the easiest day trips in Thailand. Three main options: (1) Train from Hua Lamphong (Krung Thep Aphiwat) station: 3rd class 15–20 THB (1.5–2 hrs, fan-cooled, unreserved wooden benches — the most atmospheric option), 2nd class A/C 65 THB, special express with A/C 245–345 THB (about 1 hour). Trains depart roughly hourly from early morning. (2) Minivan from Victory Monument (Mo Chit): 60–80 THB, 1–1.5 hrs depending on traffic, departures every 30 minutes 06:00–17:00. (3) Organised day tour: 1,200–2,500 THB all-in including transport, guide, lunch and temple entrance fees — convenient but inflexible. Private car/taxi: 1,500–2,500 THB one-way (1–1.5 hrs).
localTransport: Bicycle: The best way to explore. Rentals 50–100 THB/day from shops near the train station ferry and on Naresuan Road. The island is flat and compact — perfect for cycling.
Tuk-tuk: Negotiate a half-day tour (4 hours, 4–5 temples) for 800–1,200 THB. Individual trips between nearby temples 50–100 THB. Always agree on price before boarding.
Songthaew (shared pickup truck): Local routes around the island for 10–20 THB per person. Flag them down on main roads.
Long-tail boat: River tours visiting riverside temples from 1,000–1,500 THB per boat (seats 6–8 people). Arrange at the pier near the Night Market.
Ferry: Crosses the river between the train station and the island for 5 THB per person. Runs frequently throughout the day.
Motorcycle rental: 200–300 THB/day from shops near the station. Useful for reaching Bang Pa-In or temples outside the island.

mustTry: Roti sai mai — Ayutthaya's most famous street food: impossibly thin candy floss threads wrapped in a paper-thin roti pancake. Sweet, slightly salty and completely addictive. Best on Bang Ian Road near Chao Phrom Market (10–30 THB per portion).

Boat noodles (Kuay Teow Rua) — Ayutthaya is considered the birthplace of this intensely dark, rich noodle soup served in small bowls. Order 3–5 bowls for a full meal (10–15 THB per bowl). Best near the Floating Market or Rojana Road.

Giant river prawns (Kung Mae Nam) — enormous freshwater prawns from the local rivers, grilled whole over charcoal and served with spicy seafood dipping sauce. Found at riverside restaurants on the east bank (200–500 THB per portion depending on size).
Pad Thai Ayutthaya-style — the local version tends to be sweeter and served in a thin egg wrap. Try it at local restaurants on the island (50–80 THB).
Khanom Jeen Nam Ya — fermented rice noodles with fish curry sauce, served with fresh vegetables. A central Thai speciality found at morning markets (30–50 THB).
Coconut ice cream — freshly made coconut ice cream served in a coconut shell with toppings, available at the Floating Market and Night Market (30–50 THB).
foodAreas: Chao Phrom Night Market — the main evening food hub on the island with grilled meats, som tum, noodle soups and fresh fruit shakes. Open from 17:00 daily.
Bang Ian Road — the roti sai mai epicentre, with dozens of vendors hand-pulling candy floss and wrapping roti throughout the day.
Hua Raw Night Market — on the east bank near the Pa Sak River, popular with locals for seafood and river prawns.
Ayutthaya Floating Market — tourist-oriented but good for sampling roti sai mai, boat noodles, grilled seafood and coconut desserts in one place.
Rojana Road — local restaurants serving boat noodles, rice dishes and everyday Thai food at local prices.
tips: Roti sai mai is best eaten fresh — buy it on Bang Ian Road and eat immediately while the roti is warm and pliable.
Boat noodles come in tiny portions by design — do not be surprised by the bowl size. Just order more.
For river prawns, ask the price per kilogram before ordering — prices vary significantly by size.
The Night Market is the best value dinner option on the island — dishes cost 40–100 THB.
Bring cash — most food stalls and small restaurants in Ayutthaya do not accept cards.

etiquette: Temple dress code: Cover shoulders and knees at all temple ruins — even outdoor ruins enforce this at the entrance. Carry a lightweight scarf or sarong.
Buddha head at Wat Mahathat: You must crouch or kneel when photographing the Buddha head in the tree roots — your head cannot be higher than the image. Guards will ask you to do so.

Active temples: Several Ayutthaya temples (Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon, Wat Panang Choeng, Viharn Phra Mongkhon Bophit) are active places of worship — be quiet, remove shoes inside, and do not pose disrespectfully with Buddha images.
Cycling etiquette: Stay on the left side of the road (Thailand drives on the left). Give way to cars and motorbikes at intersections. Lock your bicycle when parking at temples.
Photography: Do not climb on ruins, sit on Buddha statues or point your feet at Buddha images for photos. This is a UNESCO World Heritage Site — treat it with respect.
The Monarchy: Never make critical comments about the Thai royal family — lèse-majesté is a serious criminal offence in Thailand.
Bargaining: Appropriate at the Night Market and with tuk-tuk drivers. Not appropriate at temple entrance counters, restaurants or shops with marked prices.

budget.budget: 500–1,000 THB per day (~14–29 USD): Day trip by 3rd-class train (15–20 THB each way), bicycle rental (50–100 THB), combo temple ticket (220 THB), street food meals (40–80 THB each), water and snacks. Ayutthaya is one of the cheapest day trips in Thailand. If staying overnight, add 350–600 THB for a budget guesthouse.
budget.midRange: 1,500–3,000 THB per day (~43–86 USD): 2nd-class train or minivan (65–80 THB), mid-range hotel (1,000–2,000 THB/night), bicycle rental, combo ticket, sit-down restaurant meals (100–200 THB each), river cruise (200–500 THB). Very comfortable.
budget.luxury: 4,000–8,000 THB per day (~114–229 USD): Private car transfer from Bangkok (1,500–2,500 THB), Sala Ayutthaya or iuDia (3,500–7,000 THB), private long-tail boat tour (1,500 THB), riverside dinner with river prawns (500–1,000 THB). Luxury in Ayutthaya is affordable by any international standard.
